column7: Notes
Measure word: 只 (zhī) for most animals.
Also slang for "lousy" or "weak" in some contexts.
Literally "bear-cat".
More formal: 母亲 (mǔqīn).
Maternal grandfather is 外公 (wàigōng).

column5: Nǎinai zuò de fàn hěn hǎochī.
column7: Maternal grandmother is 外婆 (wàipó).
More formal: 父亲 (fùqīn).

column5: Tā shì wǒ de hǎo péngyou.
column7: 好朋友 = "good/close friend".
Literally "again see".

Used for things and people; 美丽 (měilì) is more literary. In everyday speech, 好看 (hǎokàn, lit. "good to look at") is often more natural and casual — 漂亮 leans toward "pretty/striking", while 好看 is the all-purpose "nice-looking".
Casual; second 谢 is neutral tone.
Slightly more personal than just 谢谢.

column5: Bú xiè, zhè shì wǒ yīnggāi zuò de.
column6: Not at all, it's what I should do.
column7: 不 changes to bú before a 4th-tone syllable.
Literally "don't be polite". Tone sandhi: bù → bú.
More formal than 不好意思 (bù hǎoyìsi).
Standard reply to an apology.

column5: Nǐ hǎo, hěn gāoxìng rènshi nǐ.
column7: Both syllables 3rd tone → first becomes 2nd: ní hǎo.
Informal singular "you".
Polite/respectful form of 你.
Pluralizes pronouns and people-nouns. Always neutral tone.
你 + 们.
Same word for subject and object.
我 + 们. Inclusive form: 咱们 (zánmen).

column5: Tā yǒu yì kē shànliáng de xīn.
column7: Measure word: 颗 (kē). Also "mind/feelings".

column6: Kāng is the kāng in jiànkāng.
column7: Rarely used alone today; mostly in compounds and names.
Common toast and birthday wish.
Becomes bú before a 4th-tone syllable.
Also "to shout".
Same pronunciation as 她 and 它.
Female-specific 他; introduced in the 20th century.
Also a progressive marker: 我在吃 (I am eating).
学 (study) + 校 (school).
Both noun and verb.

column6: The teacher is in the classroom.
column7: 教 (teach) + 室 (room).

Often colloquially shortened to 啥 (shá). Typically followed by a noun (or stands alone as object): 什么书 (what book), 什么时候 (when, lit. "what time"). Contrast with 怎么 (zěnme, "how"), which is followed by a verb: 怎么去 (how to go), 怎么说 (how to say).
字 is neutral tone here.
Most common Chinese particle; always neutral tone.
我 + 的; can also stand alone meaning "mine".
你 + 的.
Used to link nouns; not used before adjectives.
Tone sandhi: bù → bú before 是 (4th tone).
Used after a surname as a respectful title.
生 is neutral tone here.
Placed at the end to turn a statement into a yes/no question.
Also used as a nationality suffix (see below).

column6: I want to travel to China.
column7: Literally "Middle Country/Kingdom".
Literally "Beautiful Country".
Phonetic transliteration.
Country + 人 = nationality.
美国 + 人.
墨西哥 + 人.
Often pronounced zhèi in colloquial speech, especially before a measure word.
Also means "to / toward" and "pair".
Tone sandhi: bù → bú before 对 (4th tone).

column5: Qǐng gěi wǒ yí ge lìzi.
column7: 子 is neutral tone here. 举例 (jǔlì) = "to give an example".

column5: Wǒ xià ge yuè qù Rìběn.
column6: I'm going to Japan next month.
column7: Literally "sun origin" / "land of the rising sun".

column6: Where are you from? (lit. which country's person)
column7: Often pronounced něi colloquially before a measure word.
Also pronounced shuí, especially in formal/literary speech.
Literally "same study". Also used by teachers to address students.

column5: Wǒ xué Hànyǔ liǎng nián le.
column6: I've studied Chinese for two years.
column7: Literally "Han language" — emphasizes the spoken language of the Han people. Common in language-learning contexts (e.g., HSK = 汉语水平考试).
Literally "Middle script" — broader, covers spoken and written Chinese. More common in everyday speech and overseas; 汉语 is more academic/standard.

column5: Zhè běn shū hěn yǒu yìsi.
column7: Measure word: 本 (běn).
Literally "English language". Parallels 汉语: more academic/standard, often used in school contexts.

column5: Zhè běn shū shì Yīngwén de.
column7: Literally "English script". Parallels 中文: covers both spoken and written, common in everyday speech, especially when referring to written material.
Literally "French language". Like 汉语/英语, slightly more formal/academic.

column5: Wǒ kàn le yì běn Fǎwén xiǎoshuō.
column7: Literally "French script". Like 中文/英文, often used for written French; interchangeable with 法语 in many contexts.
教 (teach) + 材 (material). 课本 (kèběn) is more specific to school textbooks.
Literally "Brave/Hero Country" (phonetic 英 + 国).

column5: Wǒ xià ge yuè qù Fǎguó.
column6: I'm going to France next month.
column7: Phonetic 法 + 国.
Measure word: 首 (shǒu). 唱歌 (chàng gē) = "to sing".
文 (writing/civilization) + 化 (transformation).

column5: Wǒ jiā yǒu liǎng zhī gǒu.
column7: Measure word: 只 (zhī).
Placed after a noun/pronoun to bounce a question back. Always neutral tone.
Short for 在哪里 / 在哪儿. 在 (at) + 哪 (which/where).
Short for 去哪里 / 去哪儿. 去 (go) + 哪 (which/where).
Also a measure word for businesses: 一家公司 (one company).

column5: Tā zài yì jiā dà gōngsī gōngzuò.
column6: He works at a big company.
column7: Measure word: 家 (jiā).
Indicates motion away from the speaker; opposite of 来 (lái).
The most common measure word — used when you're not sure which one fits. Pattern: number + 个 + noun. Dictionary tone is gè, but in speech it's almost always unstressed (neutral): yí ge, zhège, nàge.
Used for most animals (cats, dogs, birds), and for one of a pair (e.g., one shoe, one hand).

column5: Wǒ mǎi le sān běn shū.
column7: Used for bound items: books, notebooks, magazines.
Pair with 这 (this). Often pronounced nèi colloquially before a measure word.
这 + 个. Pattern: 这/那/哪 + measure word + (noun) to point at things.
那 + 个. Also a colloquial filler word ("um..."), pronounced nèige.
哪 + 个. The general "which one?" — works when you don't know the right measure word.

column5: Nǎ zhī gǒu shì nǐ de?
column7: 哪 + 只. Use when asking about animals (or one of a pair).

column5: Nǐ zài kàn nǎ běn shū?
column7: 哪 + 本. Use when asking about books or other bound items.
Often required (not really meaning "very") to link a subject to an adjective: 我很好 = "I'm good".

column6: I often go to the library to study.
column7: 图书 (books) + 馆 (hall/building).

column3: to look at / to read / to watch
column7: Versatile verb: 看书 (read), 看电影 (watch a movie), 看朋友 (visit a friend).
Measure word: 张 (zhāng). 拍照片 = "to take a photo".

column3: to go on a business trip
column5: Tā xià zhōu qù Běijīng chūchāi.
column6: He's going to Beijing on a business trip next week.
column7: 出 (go out) + 差 (errand/assignment). Verb-object compound — can be split: 出一次差.

column6: The Great Wall is very famous.
column7: Literally "long wall". Full name: 万里长城 (Wànlǐ Chángchéng).
星期 + number = day of the week (星期一 = Monday, 星期二 = Tuesday...). Synonym: 周 (zhōu), 礼拜 (lǐbài).

column6: On Sunday we're going to the park.
column7: Slightly more formal/written. 日 means "sun/day".

column6: What are you doing on Sunday?
column7: More colloquial than 星期日; same meaning. 天 means "day".

column4: 百分之八十的学生通过了考试。
column5: Bǎi fēn zhī bāshí de xuésheng tōngguò le kǎoshì.
column6: 80% of the students passed the exam.
column7: Pattern: 百分之 + number (the number goes AFTER, unlike English). Literally "of one hundred parts".
Tone sandhi: 一 (yī) → yí before 4th-tone 定. Often pairs with 会 (huì) for "will definitely".
Used for flat things: photos, paper, tickets, tables, beds.

column5: Wǒ mǎi le yì tiáo kùzi.
column6: I bought a pair of pants.
column7: Used for long/thin items: pants, fish, roads, rivers, dogs (sometimes), news items.

column6: This kind of tea is delicious.
column7: Means "kind/type/sort". Pattern: 这/那/哪 + 种 + noun.

column3: (measure word for pieces / chunks; colloquial for yuan)
column6: This piece of cake is delicious.
column7: Used for chunks (cake, soap, stone). Also the colloquial measure word for money (¥): 五块钱 = 5 yuan. Formal equivalent for money: 元 (yuán).
Tone sandhi: yī → yí before a 4th-tone syllable (yí ge, yí kuài); yī → yì before 1st/2nd/3rd-tone syllables (yì zhī, yì běn, yì zhāng). Stays yī when counting or at the end.
Used for counting and in compound numbers (二十 = 20). Before a measure word, use 两 (liǎng) instead: 两个人.
Used before a measure word instead of 二. Also means "a couple of" loosely.
Considered unlucky in Chinese culture because 四 (sì) sounds like 死 (sǐ, "death").
Considered lucky; sounds like 流 (liú, "flow"), associated with smoothness.
Considered the luckiest number; sounds like 发 (fā, "prosper") in some dialects.
Sounds like 久 (jiǔ, "long-lasting"); associated with longevity.
Compound numbers: 十一 (11), 十二 (12)... 二十 (20), 三十 (30).
Used in compound numbers with internal zeros: 一百零五 (105). Note: in phone numbers/addresses, 一 is often read as yāo to avoid confusion with 七.

column5: Zhè běn shū yǒu yìbǎi yè.
column7: Always used with a number: 一百 (100), 两百 (200). For numbers like 105, use 零: 一百零五.
Tone sandhi: yī → yì before 3rd-tone bǎi.

column5: Tā shì wǒ zuìhǎo de péngyou.
column7: 最 (zuì, "most") + 好 (hǎo, "good") + 的. Pattern: 最 + adjective + 的 + noun.
Also means "there is/are": 这里有一家公司 (There is a company here). Negated with 没 (méi), never with 不.
没 + 有. 有 is always negated with 没, never with 不. Colloquially shortened to 没 (méi).

column5: Wǒ de jiārén dōu hěn hǎo.
column6: My family members are all well.
column7: 家 (home/family) + 人 (person). Refers to one's family collectively or individual family members.

column5: Wǒ jiā yǒu wǔ kǒu rén.
column7: Primary meaning: "mouth" (张口 = open one's mouth). As a measure word, used specifically when counting family members: number + 口 + 人.

column6: I didn't go to school yesterday.
column7: Used to negate 有 (没有) and to negate past/completed actions (没去 = "didn't go"). Use 没 for past actions and 有; use 不 for present/future, habitual actions, willingness, and adjectives.

column3: to see / to catch sight of
column7: 看 (look) + 见 (perceive). 看 = "to look/watch" (the action of looking); 看见 = "to see" (result — you actually perceived it). 见 is a result complement.
女 (female) + 儿 (child). Son = 儿子 (érzi).

column6: How many children do you have?
column7: Used when expecting a small number (usually under 10). For larger numbers, use 多少 (duōshao).
No verb needed: 我二十岁 (not *我是二十岁). Acts as a measure word for age.

column3: many / much / more; how (to what degree)
column7: Opposite: 少 (shǎo, few/less). As an adverb before adjectives in questions, asks "how much/big/old": 多大 (how old/big), 多长 (how long), 多远 (how far).
For age, 大 means "old/grown": 多大 = "how old". Opposite: 小 (xiǎo, small/young).
At end of sentence: signals a new situation or change ("now", "already"). After a verb: marks completed action (我吃了 = "I ate"). Two distinct uses — both very common.

column6: Last year I went to China.
column7: 去 (past/gone) + 年 (year).
今 (present/now) + 年 (year).

column6: Next year I want to go to Japan.
column7: 明 (bright/next) + 年 (year).

column6: My daughter is in elementary school now.
column7: 小 (small) + 学 (study/school). In China, typically ages 6–12.

column6: He is a middle school student.
column7: 中 (middle) + 学 (study/school). Covers both 初中 (chūzhōng, junior high) and 高中 (gāozhōng, senior high).
大 (big) + 学 (study/school). 大学生 (dàxuéshēng) = "university student".
Used for ages roughly above 10. For children ~10 and under, use 几岁了 instead. More casual/general-purpose age question.
Used for children roughly 10 and under, since 几 expects a small number (under 10). For older people, use 多大了.

column3: any questions? / is there a problem?
column7: 有 (have) + 问题 (question/problem) + 吗 (question particle). Common classroom phrase.

column6: I like to eat Chinese food.
column7: 吃饭 (chī fàn) = "to eat (a meal)", literally "eat rice".

column6: What do you want to drink?
column7: 喝水 (drink water), 喝茶 (drink tea), 喝咖啡 (drink coffee).

column3: can / to know how to; will
column7: Indicates learned ability (skills). Compare: 能 (néng) = physical/situational ability; 可以 (kěyǐ) = permission. Also marks future: 我会来 (I will come).
说话 (shuō huà) = "to speak/talk" generally. 说 + language = speak that language: 说中文.
现 (present) + 在 (at). Placed at the start of a sentence or before the verb.
An adjective, so usually paired with 很: 我很饿. Opposite: 饱 (bǎo, full).
Opposite: 多 (duō, many/more). As a verb (shào), can also mean "to lack".
Opposite: 大 (dà, big). Also a familiar prefix for names: 小明 (Xiǎo Míng, "Little Ming").

column6: The teacher is in the office.
column7: 办公 (handle work) + 室 (room).

哪 + 儿 (erhua suffix). Equivalent to 哪里 (nǎlǐ). 哪儿 is more common in northern China (especially Beijing); 哪里 is more common in the south and Taiwan. Plain 哪 alone usually means "which" and needs a measure word — use 哪儿/哪里 for the standalone "where".

column6: Let's go eat at a restaurant.
column7: 饭 (meal) + 馆 (establishment) + 儿 (erhua). The 儿化 (érhuà) suffix is characteristic of Beijing speech; without it: 饭馆 (fànguǎn). Typically a smaller, casual eatery.

column5: Zhè jiā fàndiàn de cài hěn hǎochī.
column6: The food at this restaurant is delicious.
column7: 饭 (meal) + 店 (shop). Often a larger/fancier restaurant than 饭馆儿; in mainland China also commonly means "hotel" (e.g. 北京饭店).

column4: 我们需要一个新的工作方案。
column5: Wǒmen xūyào yí ge xīn de gōngzuò fāng'àn.
column6: We need a new work plan.
column7: 方 (method) + 案 (case/proposal). The apostrophe in pinyin (fāng'àn) separates the syllables so it's not read as fān-gàn. Common in business/professional contexts.
西 (west) + 安 (peace). Capital of Shaanxi (陕西) province and ancient capital of China. The apostrophe separates xī and ān to avoid reading as xiān.
儿 (child/son) + 子 (neutral tone suffix). Daughter: 女儿 (nǚ'ér).

column6: Beijing is the capital of China.
column7: 北 (north) + 京 (capital). Literally "northern capital", contrasting with 南京 (Nánjīng, "southern capital").
Three related meanings: (1) a prepared dish, (2) a cuisine (中国菜 = Chinese cuisine), (3) vegetables (买菜 = buy groceries/veggies). 点菜 (diǎn cài) = "order dishes".
写字 (xiě zì) = "to write characters". 写信 (xiě xìn) = "write a letter".
General "do/make" verb (like Spanish hacer). 做饭 (cook), 做作业 (do homework), 做工作 (do work). Compare 作 (zuò), more formal/literary, which overlaps in some compounds.

column3: to open; to drive; to turn on
column7: Many meanings: 开门 (open door), 开车 (drive), 开灯 (turn on light), 开会 (hold a meeting). Opposite of 关 (guān, close).
Measure word: 辆 (liàng). Generic term for vehicles: 汽车 (qìchē, automobile), 火车 (huǒchē, train), 自行车 (zìxíngchē, bicycle).

column6: I drive to the office every day.
column7: 开 (drive/operate) + 车 (vehicle). Verb-object compound — can be split: 开了一天车 (drove for a day).
游 (swim/travel) + 泳 (swim). Verb-object compound — can be split: 游了一会儿泳 (swam for a while). 游泳池 (yóuyǒngchí) = swimming pool.

column5: Wǒ xià ge yuè qù Xībānyá.
column6: I'm going to Spain next month.
column7: Phonetic transliteration. + 人 = Spanish person (西班牙人).
西班牙 + 语 (language). Often shortened to 西语 (Xīyǔ). Parallels 汉语 / 英语.
Formal/written form of 妈妈 (māma). Used in formal contexts, writing, or to show respect. Mother's Day = 母亲节 (Mǔqīn Jié).
Formal/written form of 爸爸 (bàba). Father's Day = 父亲节 (Fùqīn Jié).
Phonetic transliteration. + 人 = Italian (意大利人), + 语 = Italian language (意大利语).

column6: I like to eat Italian food.
column7: 意大利 (Italy) + 菜 (cuisine). Same pattern as 中国菜, 法国菜, 墨西哥菜.

做 (make) + 饭 (rice/meal). Verb-object compound — can be split: 做了一顿饭 (cooked a meal). Emphasis on preparing the meal as a whole. Near-synonym 做菜 (zuò cài) focuses on the dishes themselves. For fancier cooking: 烹饪 (pēngrèn).
做 (make) + 菜 (dish). Near-synonym of 做饭, but emphasizes the dishes/food itself rather than the meal as a whole. Often used to talk about cooking skill: 会做菜 = "knows how to cook".
好 (good) + 吃 (eat) = "good to eat". For drinks, use 好喝 (hǎohē); for things you watch/look at, 好看 (hǎokàn); for activities, 好玩 (hǎowán).
好 (good) + 看 (look) = "good to look at". More casual and natural in everyday speech than 漂亮 (piàoliang). Also used for things worth watching: 这部电影很好看 (this movie is great).
好 (good) + 玩 (play) = "good to play/do". Used for activities, games, places — anything enjoyable. 玩 here is often pronounced wánr with erhua in northern speech: hǎowánr.
More formal than 看 (kàn). 读 emphasizes reading aloud or studying (读书 = study/attend school), while 看 covers silent reading (看书 = read a book). 读 + 大学 = "attend university".

column6: How do you write this character?
column7: Refers to a single written character. Measure word: 个. Distinguish from 词 (cí, "word", which can be multiple characters).

column6: Chinese characters are hard to write.
column7: 汉 (Han) + 字 (character). The writing system used for Chinese (and historically Japanese kanji, Korean hanja).

column6: How do you read this character?
column7: Typically followed by a verb: 怎么去 (how to go), 怎么说 (how to say), 怎么写 (how to write). Contrast with 什么 (shénme, "what"), which is followed by a noun. Also asks "why/how come": 你怎么不来？ (why aren't you coming?).

column6: Mom is cooking in the kitchen.
column7: 厨 (kitchen/cook) + 房 (room). 厨师 (chúshī) = chef/cook.
